tutor_title: Why Do We Practice Mental Math in a Music Course?
tutor_p1: You’re probably wondering what solving “47 + 38” mentally has to do with playing an instrument or understanding music theory. The short answer: everything. The long answer is fascinating.
tutor_p2: When you play music, your brain is performing mental calculations all the time without you realizing it. For example, transposing a melody from C major to E-flat major is, neurologically speaking, an arithmetic operation: you are adding a constant interval to every note while reading, processing, and performing in real time. A study published in *Frontiers in Human Neuroscience* (2022) used magnetoencephalography to compare brain activity in musicians during musical transposition versus arithmetic calculations, and found that both tasks strongly activate the same frontal brain regions, particularly the left medial prefrontal cortex. Musical transposition and arithmetic share fundamental cognitive elements: both require working memory, sequential processing, and the mental adjustment of values.
tutor_p3: And that prefrontal cortex is exactly the center of all this.
tutor_h2: Dr. Kawashima and the Nintendo Game That Changed Everything
tutor_p4: In 2005, Japanese neuroscientist Ryuta Kawashima from Tohoku University did something nobody expected: he convinced Nintendo to create a video game based on his scientific research. The result was *Brain Age: Train Your Brain in Minutes a Day!* for the Nintendo DS, which sold more than 19 million copies worldwide.
tutor_p5: Kawashima’s premise was surprisingly simple, and it was backed by years of functional MRI research: performing simple arithmetic calculations quickly activates the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex more intensely than almost any other cognitive activity. This region of the brain is responsible for working memory, sustained attention, reasoning, and executive functions — exactly the abilities you need to play an instrument, read sheet music, or improvise over a harmonic progression.
tutor_p6: The counterintuitive part of Kawashima’s discovery is that *simple* tasks solved *quickly* activate the brain more than complex tasks solved slowly. The goal is not solving difficult equations; it is solving many basic operations with speed and accuracy. That’s why *Brain Age* asked players to add, subtract, and multiply small numbers against the clock instead of solving calculus problems.
tutor_h3: Arthur Benjamin and Real Mental Math Techniques
tutor_p7: If Kawashima demonstrated *why* mental math works, mathematician Arthur Benjamin showed us *how* to actually do it. In his book *Secrets of Mental Math* (written with Michael Shermer), Benjamin reveals the techniques he uses in his live performances, where he multiplies three-digit numbers mentally in front of an audience.
tutor_p8: Benjamin’s central principle can be summarized in three words: “left to right.” Contrary to the way most of us were taught in school — starting with the units and “carrying” to the left — Benjamin processes the large digits first. This is natural for the brain because it mirrors the way we process language and music: from the general structure to the details.
tutor_p9: The techniques we use in our training — complements, the distributive property, rounding squares, the ×11 trick — come directly from this book. They are not magic tricks; they are real cognitive strategies that anyone can learn.
tutor_p10: Musicians already have a natural advantage. Research published in *PLOS ONE* and the *Journal of Cognitive Neuroscience* has shown that trained musicians display greater activation in neural networks related to cognitive control and sustained attention, including the lateral prefrontal cortex and the anterior cingulate cortex. Musical training produces what neuroscientists call “cognitive transfer”: the skills developed through playing an instrument benefit other mental abilities as well.
tutor_p11: Our mental math program takes advantage of this transfer in both directions. Mental calculation strengthens exactly the same cognitive capacities you later use in music-making: working memory (holding several numbers or notes in your mind simultaneously), sequential processing (solving step by step, like reading a score measure by measure), processing speed (reacting in real time, as in improvisation), and divided attention (handling multiple elements at once).
tutor_p12: At Storm Studios Learning, mental math training does not replace musical practice; it enhances it. It’s five minutes at the beginning of each session that wake up the brain, activate the prefrontal cortex, and prepare your mind for the musical work that follows. Think of it as a musician’s warm-up — but for the brain.
tutor_p13: Each level of our “Little Elephant Math” program is designed to train a specific cognitive ability, not to turn you into a mathematician. Complements train mental flexibility. The distributive property trains the decomposition of complex problems into simple parts — exactly what you do when analyzing a chord or breaking down a complex rhythm. Mental squaring exercises train the ability to hold and manipulate information in working memory, which is exactly what happens when improvising over a harmonic structure.
tutor_p14: Mental calculation is just one piece of a broader cognitive development program that we offer at Storm Studios Learning. In the apps available on our website, we also provide memory training — and this is where the circle closes: memory is not only fundamental for any musician (retaining melodic phrases, harmonic progressions, lyrics, and song structures), but also indispensable for mental calculation itself. When you solve a two-digit by two-digit multiplication mentally, you must remember partial results while calculating the next step. Without trained working memory, longer operations simply collapse halfway through. In this way, memory training and mental calculation feed each other: memory allows you to perform more complex operations, and more complex operations exercise your memory. Together, they form a complete system of cognitive training that directly complements your musical development.
tutor_p15: Science supports it. Practice confirms it. And if, along the way, you can impress someone by multiplying 85 × 11 in your head in three seconds… well, that’s nice too.
